turns.content: I took Tali out walking on country yesterday to show her some bush tucker. She wants to know the English names for things too so she can look them up. Can you help me match some up.
Of course, I'd be happy to help match traditional bush foods with their English or botanical names. What have you been showing her?

We found green plums yesterday. The small sour ones that grow on the trees near the creek. She ate about ten of them straight off the tree. And there were bush potatoes in the sandy ground near the ridge. She was proper keen to dig them out.

The green plums you're describing sound like Kakadu plums -- *Terminalia ferdinandiana*. They're famous for having extremely high vitamin C content, much more than oranges. The sour taste is a giveaway.

The bush potatoes from sandy ground are likely *Ipomoea costata* or possibly *Vigna lanceolata* depending on the shape. Did they look like small round tubers or more elongated?
Round ones, about the size of a golf ball. Light brown skin. We cooked them in the coals and they went soft and sweet inside. Tali said they tasted like sweet potato but better.

That sounds like *Ipomoea costata* -- the desert yam or bush potato. They're in the same family as sweet potato so Tali's comparison is spot on. Cooking them in coals is the traditional way and brings out that sweetness.

What else did you come across? This time of year in the Top End there should be quite a bit around.

We saw water lilies in the billabong. The stems and the seeds are both good to eat. I showed her how to pull the stems and eat the crunchy part near the base. And there were some pandanus nuts but they are a lot of work to get into.

The water lilies would be *Nymphaea* species -- likely *Nymphaea violacea* or *Nymphaea macrosperma* in that region. The stems, roots, and seeds are all edible. The seeds can be ground into flour too.

Pandanus nuts -- *Pandanus spiralis* -- are definitely labour-intensive. You have to crack the hard outer casing to get to the kernel inside. Did you show Tali the technique for opening them, or save that for another day?

We had a go at a few but her hands got sore from cracking them. I told her the old people used to do it with rocks, proper technique, find the right shaped stone and use it as a tool. She wants to come back with a rock next time and try again. She is stubborn like that which is a good thing.

Stubbornness is a good quality when it comes to learning skills that take patience. Using a stone tool is a much better approach than bare hands -- the right rock makes all the difference.

Is Tali picking up the identification side of things? Knowing which plants are safe to eat and which to avoid is the most important part.

She is getting better. I teach her the way I was taught. You learn by walking with someone who knows. Not from a book. She knows the green plums now and the water lily. The bush potato she needs to practice finding because you have to read the ground, look for the vine and the cracks in the soil where the tuber is pushing up. That takes time to see.

Reading the ground like that is a skill that only comes from practice -- learning to see the subtle signs. The vine and the soil cracks are clues that wouldn't mean anything to someone who hadn't been shown what to look for. Are there any plants around there that she needs to know to avoid? Ones that look similar to edible species but aren't safe?

There are a few. Some of the berries look proper tempting but will make you sick. I teach her the rule my aunties taught me. If you do not know it, you do not eat it. Full stop. No guessing. No trying a little bit. You either know or you leave it alone. She listens to that one.

That's a clear, simple rule that could save her life. No grey area. It's the same principle used in foraging traditions worldwide -- certainty or nothing. The fact that she listens to it shows she's taking the learning seriously.

She does take it seriously. She is at the age where she could go either way, get pulled into the phone and the internet or stay connected to country. I want her to have both but the country knowledge has to be there first. Once it is in you it does not leave. She asked me to take her out again this weekend so that is a good sign.

That's a really good sign -- the fact that she's asking to go back rather than being dragged along. You're right that the country knowledge is foundational. If she builds that base now, she'll carry it regardless of what else comes into her life. Enjoy the weekend walk with her.
